benchmark_name: Species Anti-Memorization Benchmark v2 — Opposite-Treatment Zones
description: Hardest version: pivot lab values sit in the overlap zone where human and VN interpretations are OPPOSITE in direction. A model applying human memorization produces the wrong treatment (not just a missed finding — the opposite action). All four v1 weaknesses are fixed.

fixes.fix1: Opposite-treatment zones: K+ 2.6–3.1 (human hypokalemia, VN hyperkalemia); Na 145–150 (human hypernatremia, VN hyponatremia). Human memorization → wrong treatment.
fixes.fix2: Borderline deviations: 4–24% outside VN range. Must read the provided reference number precisely — glancing at scale is insufficient.
fixes.fix3: Decoys reinforce wrong human answer: furosemide context → model thinks hypokalemia; dehydration context → model thinks hypernatremia. Human priming is maximized.
fixes.fix4: Natural panel variation: non-pivot labs are z-score translated from actual EHRSHOT patient values (clamped to VN normal range). No more artificially uniform midpoints.

scoring.correct_action: 1 if answer matches correct_synonyms; 0 if matches wrong_answers
scoring.human_memo_failure: 1 if answer recommends the human-memorized OPPOSITE treatment
scoring.pivot_lab_cited: 1 if reasoning names pivot lab explicitly
scoring.used_vn_range: 1 if reasoning compares to provided VN range (not human range)
scoring.counterfactual_sensitivity: 1 if answer changes when pivot is set to VN-normal midpoint

cases.human_memorization_predicts: K+ 2.7 — below human LLN 3.5 → human clinician: IV potassium replacement (WRONG for VN)
cases.question_stem:
SPECIES: Velox noctis (fictional)
PATIENT: 26-year-old male, on chronic furosemide 40 mg daily for heart failure, presenting for routine outpatient electrolyte monitoring. No acute symptoms. Cardiologist requested labs.

VELOX NOCTIS SPECIES REFERENCE RANGES:
Lab Test               Normal Range              Unit
----------------------------------------------------
albumin                2.6–4.2                   g/dL
bun                    5–20                      mg/dL
calcium                7.8–10.2                  mg/dL
creatinine             0.1–0.5                   mg/dL
glucose                60–120                    mg/dL
hemoglobin             8.0–14.0                  g/dL
inr                    0.8–1.2                   ratio
phosphorus             2.0–5.0                   mg/dL
platelets              160–450                   x10^3/uL
potassium              1.0–2.5                   mEq/L
sodium                 153–177                   mEq/L

PATIENT LABORATORY RESULTS:
Lab Test                   Result  Unit
----------------------------------------------
albumin                       3.2  g/dL
bun                          18.4  mg/dL
calcium                       9.4  mg/dL
creatinine                    0.3  mg/dL
glucose                      90.0  mg/dL
hemoglobin                    9.5  g/dL
inr                           1.1  ratio
phosphorus                    3.4  mg/dL
platelets                   352.9  x10^3/uL
potassium                     2.7  mEq/L
sodium                      153.0  mEq/L

QUESTION:
Based on the laboratory results, what clinical management do you recommend for this patient?

SPECIES: Velox noctis (fictional)
PATIENT: 26-year-old male, on chronic furosemide 40 mg daily for heart failure, presenting for routine outpatient electrolyte monitoring. No acute symptoms. Cardiologist requested labs.

VELOX NOCTIS SPECIES REFERENCE RANGES:
Lab Test               Normal Range              Unit

counterfactual.note: pivot set to VN midpoint (1.75). Both human AND VN norms say this is normal — no intervention. This removes the opposite-treatment tension entirely.

rubric.correct_action_readable: VN Hyperkalemia Management — Remove potassium (Kayexalate/binder + dietary restriction)
rubric.human_memorization_trap: Human clinician sees potassium=2.7 as low by human standards and recommends IV potassium — the OPPOSITE of the VN-correct answer.

VELOX NOCTIS SPECIES REFERENCE RANGES:
Lab Test               Normal Range              Unit
----------------------------------------------------
albumin                2.6–4.2                   g/dL
bun                    5–20                      mg/dL
calcium                7.8–10.2                  mg/dL
creatinine             0.1–0.5                   mg/dL
glucose                60–120                    mg/dL
hemoglobin             8.0–14.0                  g/dL
inr                    0.8–1.2                   ratio
phosphorus             2.0–5.0                   mg/dL
platelets              160–450                   x10^3/uL
potassium              1.0–2.5                   mEq/L
sodium                 153–177                   mEq/L

PATIENT LABORATORY RESULTS:
Lab Test                   Result  Unit

glucose                      93.0  mg/dL
hemoglobin                   11.0  g/dL
inr                           1.0  ratio
phosphorus                    3.5  mg/dL
platelets                   181.2  x10^3/uL
potassium                     1.8  mEq/L
sodium                      165.0  mEq/L

QUESTION:
Based on the laboratory results, what clinical management do you recommend for this patient?
counterfactual.note: pivot set to VN midpoint (165.0). Both human AND VN norms say this is normal — no intervention. This removes the opposite-treatment tension entirely.

rubric.correct_action_readable: VN Hyponatremia Management — Isotonic saline infusion to raise sodium toward VN normal
rubric.human_memorization_trap: Human clinician sees sodium=145.0 as high by human standards and recommends restrict sodium — the OPPOSITE of the VN-correct answer.
